sanskrit_devanagari:
आरुरुक्षोर्मुनेर्योगं कर्म कारणमुच्यते ।
योगारूढस्य तस्यैव शमः कारणमुच्यते ॥ ३ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
ārurukṣor muner yogaṁ karma kāraṇam ucyate |
yogārūḍhasya tasyaiva śamaḥ kāraṇam ucyate || 3 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: For one who is a neophyte in the eightfold yoga system, work is said to be the means; and for one who is already elevated in yoga, cessation of all material activities is said to be the means.
For one who is a neophyte in the eightfold yoga system, work is said to be the means; and for one who is already elevated in yoga, cessation of all material activities is said to be the means.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1301 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The process of linking oneself with the Supreme is called yoga. It may be compared to a ladder for attaining the topmost spiritual realization. This ladder begins from the lowest material condition of the living entity and rises up to perfect self-realization in pure spiritual life.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.3 Aruruksoh, for one who wishes to ascend, who has not ascended, i.e. for that very person who is unable to remain established in Dhyana-yoga;-for which person who is desirous to ascend?-munch, for the sage, i.e.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-3-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: 6.3 Of the man of meditation who is desirous of ascending, who has not attained to steadiness in yoga, karma is said to be the means; but of the same man when he has ascended yoga, sama, withdrawal from all karmas, is said to be the means.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.3 Karma Yoga is said to be the means for an aspirant for release who ‘seeks to climb the heights of Yoga,’ i.e., the vision of the self. For the same person, when he has climbed the ‘heights of Yoga,’ i.e., when he is established in Yoga — tranquility, i.e., freedom from actions is said…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-3-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: 6.3 For one who is yet to ascend in yoga, action without attachment is the means; for one who has ascended, the calm of the yoked mind, free from agitation, is the means by which higher yoga proceeds.

positions.rendering: withdrawal from all karmas, complete cessation of active engagement
calm of the yoked mind, freedom from agitation, by which higher yoga proceeds

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Major sectarian divergence. Shankara reads śama as withdrawal-from-action (the ascended yogi has graduated past karma-yoga into pure jñāna-niṣṭhā). Ramanuja reads it operationally as the calm-of-the-yoked-mind, still within the karma-yoga frame. Prabhupada follows Shankara's reading.

literal_meaning: For the muni desiring to ascend yoga, action (karma) is said to be the means; for that same one ascended in yoga, calm (śama) is said to be the means.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna distinguishes two stages of the practitioner. The aspirant climbing toward yoga has action as the means of ascent. The same person, once established in yoga, has calm — cessation of agitation — as the operative means. The verse is structural: it explains why the chapter will move shortly from action-instruction to meditation-instruction. Shankara emphasises the staged reading; Ramanuja reads it as a continuum within karma-yoga itself.
